subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Have you ever wondered what makes a car move, what makes a light bulb glow, or what gives you the strength to run and play? The answer to all of these questions is one amazing word: energy. Energy is everywhere around you, all the time. When you wake up in the morning and the sun rises over Mogadishu, that is energy. When your mother cooks bariis (rice) over a charcoal fire, that is energy. When you kick a football across the sand, that is energy too. In this unit, you will discover what energy really is, the many different forms it takes, and how it moves from place to place to make things happen.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what energy is and why it is needed for anything to happen.
Name and describe the main forms of energy: light, heat, sound, movement (kinetic), stored (potential), and electrical energy.
Identify different energy sources, such as the Sun, food, wind, moving water, charcoal, and fuel.
Describe how energy is transferred from one object to another and how it changes from one form to another.
State the big rule of energy: energy cannot be created or destroyed, only changed or moved.
Explain the difference between renewable energy (like solar and wind) and non-renewable energy (like oil and charcoal).
Give practical examples of how to save energy in your own home and community.
Investigate energy questions fairly and record what you observe.
Before we begin, let us meet the important words of this unit. Do not try to memorise them all at once. You will meet each of them again and again, and soon they will feel like old friends.
The ability to do work or cause a change.

concepts.title: What Exactly Is Energy?
concepts.explanation: Let us start with the biggest question of all: what is energy? Scientists have a short answer. Energy is the ability to do work or to make something change. That sounds simple, but let us think carefully about what it means. When something has energy, it can push, pull, lift, heat, light up, or make a sound. Without energy, nothing at all would happen. The whole world would be frozen and still, like a photograph that never moves. Here is a helpful way to think about it. Imagine your friend Amina wants to carry a…
concepts.example: One of the most important things to understand is that you cannot usually see energy itself. You cannot hold a handful of energy the way you can hold a handful of sand. Instead, you see what energy does. When you see a…

concepts.title: The Many Forms of Energy
concepts.explanation: Energy is like water: it can take many different shapes. Just as water can be a solid block of ice, a flowing liquid, or invisible steam, energy comes in many different forms. Let us meet each one, because being able to name the forms of energy is one of the most useful skills a young scientist can learn. Light energy is the form of energy that lets you see the world. It travels in straight lines and moves incredibly fast, faster than anything else in the universe. Our greatest source of light energy is the Sun.…
concepts.example: Heat energy, which scientists call thermal energy, is the form of energy that makes things warm or hot. You feel heat energy from the sun on a hot afternoon in Garissa. You feel it from the glowing charcoal when your…

concepts.title: Where Does Energy Come From? Energy Sources
concepts.explanation: Every bit of energy you use comes from somewhere. The place that energy comes from is called an energy source. Let us explore the most important energy sources, especially the ones that matter here in East Africa. The greatest energy source of all is the Sun. This may surprise you, but almost all the energy on Earth begins with the Sun. Sunlight gives us light and warmth directly. But the Sun also helps plants grow, and plants store the Sun's energy inside them. When a goat eats grass, it takes in that stored…
concepts.example: Food is the energy source for all living things, including you. Foods like bariis (rice), dates, canjeero, camel milk, bananas, and beans all contain chemical potential energy. Your body is like a wonderful machine that…

concepts.title: Energy on the Move: Transfers and Changes
concepts.explanation: One of the most exciting things about energy is that it never stays still. Energy is always on the move, jumping from one object to another and changing from one form to another. When energy moves from one object to another, we call it an energy transfer. When energy changes from one form into a different form, we call it an energy transformation. These two ideas are at the very heart of understanding energy. Let us follow the energy on a real journey. Think about your friend Yusuf kicking a football on the sand.…
concepts.example: Here is another wonderful example that shows energy changing back and forth. Imagine a swing in a playground, or a pendulum, which is just a weight hanging on a string. When you pull the swing back and hold it high, it…

concepts.title: The Great Rule: Energy Cannot Be Created or Destroyed
concepts.explanation: Now we come to one of the most important rules in all of science. It is called the conservation of energy, and it says something amazing: energy cannot be created out of nothing, and it cannot be destroyed. It can only be changed from one form to another, or moved from one object to another. This rule is always true, everywhere in the universe, with no exceptions. This can be a little confusing at first. You might ask: if energy is never destroyed, why does a swing eventually stop? Why does a rolling ball come to…
concepts.example: The rubbing that slows things down is caused by a force called friction. Friction turns useful movement energy into heat energy that spreads away. This is why no machine can keep moving forever on its own. Some people…

concepts.title: Renewable Energy and Saving Energy
concepts.explanation: Some energy sources will run out one day, and some will not. This is a very important difference. Non-renewable energy sources, such as oil, coal, and natural gas, took millions of years to form deep inside the Earth. Once we use them up, they are gone forever, and they can also make the air dirty when they burn. Renewable energy sources, on the other hand, will not run out. The Sun will keep shining for billions of years, the wind will keep blowing, and rivers will keep flowing. This makes renewable energy very…
concepts.example: Wind energy is another clean, renewable source. Along the coast near Berbera and Mombasa, and across the open plains, the wind blows strongly. Tall machines called wind turbines have huge blades that the wind pushes…

explorations.title: The Swinging Pendulum (Kinetic and Potential Energy)
explorations.context: To watch how energy changes back and forth between stored (potential) energy and moving (kinetic) energy.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down your prediction. Where do you think the pendulum will move fastest, and where will it move slowest? Why do you think so?
explorations.answer: At which point did the pendulum have the most potential (stored) energy? At which point did it have the most kinetic (movement) energy? Why do you think the pendulum eventually stopped swinging? Was your hypothesis correct? Explain what you found.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch how energy changes back and forth between stored (potential) energy and moving (kinetic) energy.

explorations.title: The Sunshine Water Warmer (Light and Heat Energy)
explorations.context: To show that light energy from the Sun can be changed into heat energy, and that dark colours soak up more heat than light colours.
explorations.prompt: Which cup of water do you think will get warmer in the sunshine: the one wrapped in dark cloth or the one wrapped in white cloth? Write your prediction.
explorations.answer: Which cup became warmer? Was your hypothesis correct? What form of energy came from the Sun, and what form did it change into? Why do you think the dark cloth made a difference? How could you use this idea to warm water for washing using only the Sun?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that light energy from the Sun can be changed into heat energy, and that dark colours soak up more heat than light colours.

explorations.title: The Rubber Band Launcher (Elastic Potential Energy)
explorations.context: To show that a stretched rubber band stores energy, and that stretching it more stores more energy.
explorations.prompt: If you stretch the rubber band further back, do you think a paper ball will fly a shorter or a longer distance? Write your prediction and your reason.
explorations.answer: What kind of stored energy is in the stretched rubber band? What happened to the distance when you stretched the band further? Why? When you let go, what did the stored energy change into? Was your hypothesis correct?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that a stretched rubber band stores energy, and that stretching it more stores more energy.

explorations.title: The Pinwheel and the Wind (Kinetic Energy of Air)
explorations.context: To show that moving air (wind) carries kinetic energy that can do work by turning a pinwheel.
explorations.prompt: Do you think the pinwheel will spin faster in a gentle breeze or in a strong wind? Write your prediction.
explorations.answer: What made the pinwheel turn? What kind of energy does the moving air carry? When did the pinwheel spin fastest? What does this tell you about strong wind? How is your pinwheel like a giant wind turbine?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show that moving air (wind) carries kinetic energy that can do work by turning a pinwheel.

methods.title: The Swinging Pendulum (Kinetic and Potential Energy)
methods.example: To watch how energy changes back and forth between stored (potential) energy and moving (kinetic) energy.
steps: Tie the heavy object firmly to one end of the string.
Tie or hold the other end of the string to a fixed point so the object can swing freely.
Pull the object gently to one side so it is raised up, and hold it still. Notice that it is now higher up.
Let go (do not push) and watch the object swing back and forth.
Watch closely: at which point does it move fastest? At which points does it pause?

methods.title: The Sunshine Water Warmer (Light and Heat Energy)
methods.example: To show that light energy from the Sun can be changed into heat energy, and that dark colours soak up more heat than light colours.
steps: Fill both cups with the same amount of water at the same temperature.
Wrap one cup in dark cloth and the other in white cloth.
If you have a thermometer, measure and write down the starting temperature of each cup.
Place both cups side by side in a sunny spot.
Wait for about one hour. Try to keep everything else the same, so it is a fair test.

methods.title: The Rubber Band Launcher (Elastic Potential Energy)
methods.example: To show that a stretched rubber band stores energy, and that stretching it more stores more energy.
steps: Hook the rubber band over your thumb, and place the paper ball in the band like a little sling.
Pull the band back only a small amount (for example, 5 cm) and let go, aiming at the open floor, never at anyone.
Measure how far the paper ball travelled and write it down.
Now pull the band back further (for example, 10 cm) and launch again. Measure the distance.
Try a third, even bigger stretch. Measure again.

methods.title: The Pinwheel and the Wind (Kinetic Energy of Air)
methods.example: To show that moving air (wind) carries kinetic energy that can do work by turning a pinwheel.
steps: Fold the paper square corner to corner both ways to find the centre, then unfold it.
Carefully cut along each fold from the corner towards the centre, but stop before you reach the middle.
Bring every other corner into the centre to form the pinwheel shape, and pin them at the middle.
Pin the pinwheel loosely onto the handle so it can spin freely.
Blow on it gently and watch it spin. Then blow harder.

workedExamples.title: 1 - Following the Energy of a Falling Mango
workedExamples.prompt: A ripe mango hangs high in a tree. Suddenly the stalk breaks and the mango falls to the ground with a thud. Let us describe the energy changes.
workedExamples.solution: Start: While the mango is high up and still, it has stored gravitational potential energy because of its height. Falling: As it falls, its height gets smaller, so the potential energy changes into kinetic energy. The mango moves faster and faster. The thud: When it hits the ground, the kinetic energy is transferred to the ground and the air, turning into a little sound energy (the thud) and a tiny bit of heat energy. Conclusion: No energy was lost. It simply changed form, from potential to kinetic to sound and…

workedExamples.title: 2 - Comparing Kinetic Energy
workedExamples.prompt: Two friends roll balls across the sand. Amina rolls her ball slowly. Farah rolls a ball of the same size much faster. Which ball has more kinetic energy?
workedExamples.solution: Remember the rule: The faster something moves, the more kinetic energy it has. Compare: Both balls are the same size, so weight is not the difference. The only difference is speed. Answer: Farah's faster ball has more kinetic energy. That is why it would roll further and be harder to stop. Worked Example 3 - The Solar Lamp

workedExamples.title: 3 - The Solar Lamp
workedExamples.prompt: Khadija has a small solar lamp. During the day she leaves it in the sunshine, and at night it glows so she can study. Describe the energy journey.
workedExamples.solution: Daytime: Light energy from the Sun hits the solar panel on the lamp. Storing: The panel changes the light energy into electrical energy, which is stored in the lamp's small battery as chemical energy. Night-time: When Khadija switches it on, the stored energy changes back into electrical energy and then into light energy so she can see. Conclusion: The Sun's free energy was collected, stored, and used later. This is renewable energy at work.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The greatest source of energy for the Earth is: (a) charcoal (b) the Sun (c) batteries (d) wind
workedExamples.solution: (b) the Sun. Almost all energy on Earth begins with the Sun, including food and wind.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a renewable energy source? (a) oil (b) coal (c) solar energy (d) petrol
workedExamples.solution: (c) solar energy. The Sun will not run out, so solar energy is renewable. Oil, coal and petrol are non-renewable.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: When a mango falls from a tree, its potential energy changes into: (a) sound energy only (b) kinetic energy (c) electrical energy (d) light energy
workedExamples.solution: (b) kinetic energy. As the mango falls, its stored (potential) energy becomes movement (kinetic) energy.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Energy can be: (a) created from nothing (b) destroyed completely (c) changed from one form to another (d) made to vanish
workedExamples.solution: (c) changed from one form to another. Energy is never created or destroyed, only changed or moved.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: What form of energy do we hear with our ears? (a) light energy (b) sound energy (c) heat energy (d) electrical energy
workedExamples.solution: (b) sound energy. We hear sound energy, which travels as vibrations through the air.

practice.prompt: Energy is best described as: (a) a kind of food (b) the ability to do work or cause change (c) a type of metal (d) a colour
practice.answer: (b) the ability to do work or cause change. This is the scientific meaning of energy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which type of energy does a running goat have? (a) light energy (b) sound energy (c) kinetic energy (d) chemical energy
practice.answer: (c) kinetic energy. Anything that moves, like a running goat, has kinetic (movement) energy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What type of energy is stored in a stretched rubber band? (a) elastic potential energy (b) light energy (c) sound energy (d) thermal energy
practice.answer: (a) elastic potential energy. Stretching stores energy that is released when you let go.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The greatest source of energy for the Earth is: (a) charcoal (b) the Sun (c) batteries (d) wind
practice.answer: (b) the Sun. Almost all energy on Earth begins with the Sun, including food and wind.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a renewable energy source? (a) oil (b) coal (c) solar energy (d) petrol
practice.answer: (c) solar energy. The Sun will not run out, so solar energy is renewable. Oil, coal and petrol are non-renewable.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When a mango falls from a tree, its potential energy changes into: (a) sound energy only (b) kinetic energy (c) electrical energy (d) light energy
practice.answer: (b) kinetic energy. As the mango falls, its stored (potential) energy becomes movement (kinetic) energy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Energy can be: (a) created from nothing (b) destroyed completely (c) changed from one form to another (d) made to vanish
practice.answer: (c) changed from one form to another. Energy is never created or destroyed, only changed or moved.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What form of energy do we hear with our ears? (a) light energy (b) sound energy (c) heat energy (d) electrical energy
practice.answer: (b) sound energy. We hear sound energy, which travels as vibrations through the air.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A solar panel changes light energy into: (a) sound energy (b) electrical energy (c) chemical energy (d) potential energy
practice.answer: (b) electrical energy. A solar panel turns the Sun's light energy into electricity.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which food gives your body energy? (a) a stone (b) dates and bariis (c) a plastic cup (d) sand
practice.answer: (b) dates and bariis. Food holds chemical energy that our bodies use.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why does a rolling ball slow down and stop? (a) energy is destroyed (b) energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out (c) it runs out of colour (d) the Sun stops shining
practice.answer: (b) its movement energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out. The energy is not destroyed, only scattered.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these carries kinetic energy? (a) the wind on the coast (b) a still rock on the ground (c) a switched-off lamp (d) a cold cup of tea
practice.answer: (a) the wind on the coast. Moving air has kinetic energy; the still and switched-off things do not.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Swinging Pendulum (Kinetic and Potential Energy)
activities.materials: A piece of string or strong thread, about 50 cm long; A small heavy object such as a metal washer, a bunch of keys, or a small stone in a cloth bag; A fixed point to hang it from, such as a door handle, a hook, or the back of a chair
steps: Tie the heavy object firmly to one end of the string.
Tie or hold the other end of the string to a fixed point so the object can swing freely.
Pull the object gently to one side so it is raised up, and hold it still. Notice that it is now higher up.
Let go (do not push) and watch the object swing back and forth.
Watch closely: at which point does it move fastest? At which points does it pause?

activities.title: The Sunshine Water Warmer (Light and Heat Energy)
activities.materials: Two identical cups or small jars; The same amount of water in each cup (fill them equally); A piece of dark cloth or dark paper, and a piece of white or light cloth or paper; A sunny spot outdoors or by a window; Optional: a thermometer if you have one, or just use your finger to feel
steps: Fill both cups with the same amount of water at the same temperature.
Wrap one cup in dark cloth and the other in white cloth.
If you have a thermometer, measure and write down the starting temperature of each cup.
Place both cups side by side in a sunny spot.
Wait for about one hour. Try to keep everything else the same, so it is a fair test.

activities.title: The Rubber Band Launcher (Elastic Potential Energy)
activities.materials: A rubber band; A small light object to launch, such as a scrunched-up ball of paper; A measuring tape, a ruler, or a length of string you can mark; A clear, open floor space with nothing breakable nearby
steps: Hook the rubber band over your thumb, and place the paper ball in the band like a little sling.
Pull the band back only a small amount (for example, 5 cm) and let go, aiming at the open floor, never at anyone.
Measure how far the paper ball travelled and write it down.
Now pull the band back further (for example, 10 cm) and launch again. Measure the distance.
Try a third, even bigger stretch. Measure again.

activities.title: The Pinwheel and the Wind (Kinetic Energy of Air)
activities.materials: A square of paper (about 15 cm by 15 cm); A pin, a thumbtack, or a thin stick to hold the centre; A pencil with an eraser, a straw, or a stick to act as the handle; Scissors (ask an adult to help with cutting)
steps: Fold the paper square corner to corner both ways to find the centre, then unfold it.
Carefully cut along each fold from the corner towards the centre, but stop before you reach the middle.
Bring every other corner into the centre to form the pinwheel shape, and pin them at the middle.
Pin the pinwheel loosely onto the handle so it can spin freely.
Blow on it gently and watch it spin. Then blow harder.

activities.title: Observe: The Great Rule: Energy Cannot Be Created or Destroyed
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the great rule: energy cannot be created or destroyed around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Renewable Energy and Saving Energy
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of renewable energy and saving energy around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: the rule: The faster something moves, the more kinetic energy it has.

terms: The ability to do work or cause a change.
The energy of a moving object.
Stored energy ready to be used.
Energy that lets us see and travels from bright things.
Energy of vibrations that we hear.
Energy moving from one object to another.
Energy changing from one form to another.
A force that slows moving things and makes heat.
Energy that will not run out.

vocabulary.meaning: The ability to do work or cause a change.
vocabulary.example: Unit Overview - Welcome to the World of Energy

vocabulary.meaning: The energy of a moving object.
vocabulary.example: When you see a boat moving across the water, you are seeing kinetic energy at work.

vocabulary.meaning: Stored energy ready to be used.
vocabulary.example: Potential energy is stored energy that is waiting to be used.
When you feel the warmth of the sun on your skin, you are feeling thermal energy.

vocabulary.meaning: Energy that lets us see and travels from bright things.
vocabulary.example: Light energy is the form of energy that lets you see the world.

vocabulary.meaning: Energy of vibrations that we hear.
vocabulary.example: When you hear the call to prayer travel across the town, you are hearing sound energy.
Electrical energy is the energy that flows through wires to power the machines and devices we use.
The place that energy comes from is called an energy source.

vocabulary.meaning: Energy moving from one object to another.
vocabulary.example: When energy moves from one object to another, we call it an energy transfer.

vocabulary.meaning: Energy changing from one form to another.
vocabulary.example: When energy changes from one form into a different form, we call it an energy transformation.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that slows moving things and makes heat.
vocabulary.example: The rubbing that slows things down is caused by a force called friction.

vocabulary.meaning: Energy that will not run out.
vocabulary.example: Explain the difference between renewable energy (like solar and wind) and non-renewable energy (like oil and charcoal).

commonMistakes: Thinking energy disappears when things stop.
It looks like it vanishes, but energy is never destroyed.
Thinking only moving things have energy.
Still things can hold stored (potential) energy too.
Thinking heavy always means more energy.
Speed matters too, not just weight.
Wall electricity is high-voltage and dangerous.
Believing a machine can make free energy.

connections.text: Electrical energy, circuits, and batteries build directly on this unit.
Light and sound are both forms of energy you will study more deeply.
Food gives living things chemical energy; the Sun powers plants through photosynthesis.
The Sun drives weather, wind, and the seasons with its energy.
Solar and wind energy are important for communities across East Africa.
Measuring, recording, and comparing energy data uses counting and simple graphs.

fluency.prompt: Energy is best described as: (a) a kind of food (b) the ability to do work or cause change (c) a type of metal (d) a colour
fluency.answer: (b) the ability to do work or cause change. This is the scientific meaning of energy.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.errorFeedback: (b) sound energy. We hear sound energy, which travels as vibrations through the air.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) electrical energy. A solar panel turns the Sun's light energy into electricity.

fluency.prompt: Which food gives your body energy? (a) a stone (b) dates and bariis (c) a plastic cup (d) sand
fluency.answer: (b) dates and bariis. Food holds chemical energy that our bodies use.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) dates and bariis. Food holds chemical energy that our bodies use.

fluency.prompt: Why does a rolling ball slow down and stop? (a) energy is destroyed (b) energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out (c) it runs out of colour (d) the Sun stops shining
fluency.answer: (b) its movement energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out. The energy is not destroyed, only scattered.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) its movement energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out. The energy is not destroyed, only scattered.

fluency.prompt: Which of these carries kinetic energy? (a) the wind on the coast (b) a still rock on the ground (c) a switched-off lamp (d) a cold cup of tea
fluency.answer: (a) the wind on the coast. Moving air has kinetic energy; the still and switched-off things do not.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (a) the wind on the coast. Moving air has kinetic energy; the still and switched-off things do not.

realProblems.prompt: Halima's family lives far from any power station. Explain why solar panels on their roof would be very useful for them.
realProblems.answer: Solar panels make electricity from sunlight without needing a power station or wires from far away. Since East Africa is very sunny, Halima's family could light their home and charge devices using free, clean, renewable energy right where they live.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Solar panels make electricity from sunlight without needing a power station or wires from far away. Since East Africa is very sunny, Halima's family could light their home and charge devices using free, clean, renewable energy right where they live.

realProblems.prompt: Omar boils water from the well before drinking it. What form of energy makes the water safe, and where does that energy come from if he uses a charcoal stove?
realProblems.answer: Heat (thermal) energy makes the water safe by killing germs. If Omar uses a charcoal stove, that heat energy comes from the chemical energy stored in the charcoal, which is released when it burns.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Heat (thermal) energy makes the water safe by killing germs. If Omar uses a charcoal stove, that heat energy comes from the chemical energy stored in the charcoal, which is released when it burns.

realProblems.prompt: The coast near Berbera is very windy. Explain how this wind could be used to make electricity.
realProblems.answer: The wind carries kinetic energy. If it pushes the blades of a wind turbine, the turbine spins and changes the wind's movement energy into electrical energy for homes.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The wind carries kinetic energy. If it pushes the blades of a wind turbine, the turbine spins and changes the wind's movement energy into electrical energy for homes.

realProblems.prompt: Aisha eats a good breakfast of canjeero and dates before school. Explain how this food gives her energy to learn and play.
realProblems.answer: The canjeero and dates contain chemical potential energy. Aisha's body unlocks this stored energy and uses it to power her muscles and brain, giving her the energy to learn and play.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The canjeero and dates contain chemical potential energy. Aisha's body unlocks this stored energy and uses it to power her muscles and brain, giving her the energy to learn and play.

realProblems.prompt: During the day, the sun is bright. Explain why using daylight instead of switching on lamps helps save energy.
realProblems.answer: Daylight is free energy from the Sun. Using it instead of lamps means no electricity or fuel is used, so energy and money are saved and nothing is wasted.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Daylight is free energy from the Sun. Using it instead of lamps means no electricity or fuel is used, so energy and money are saved and nothing is wasted.

realProblems.prompt: A man claims he has built a machine that runs forever and makes more energy than it uses. Using the great rule of energy, explain why this is impossible.
realProblems.answer: It is impossible because energy cannot be created from nothing. A machine can only pass along the energy it is given, and some of that energy always leaks away as heat because of friction. So no machine can ever give out more energy than it takes in.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: It is impossible because energy cannot be created from nothing. A machine can only pass along the energy it is given, and some of that energy always leaks away as heat because of friction. So no machine can ever give out more energy than it takes in.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In your own words, explain what energy is.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Energy is the ability to do work or to make something change or happen. Accept any answer with this meaning.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the six main forms of energy you have learned.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The six main forms are: light, heat (thermal), sound, movement (kinetic), stored (potential), and electrical energy.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one local example of heat energy and one local example of light energy.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Heat energy example: a charcoal stove, the Sun's warmth, or hot tea. Light energy example: sunlight, a torch, a lamp, or a lamp flame. Accept any correct local example.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between renewable and non-renewable energy? Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Renewable energy will not run out (example: solar or wind). Non-renewable energy will run out one day (example: oil, coal, or natural gas).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what happens to the energy when you kick a football and it rolls to a stop.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Your foot transfers kinetic energy to the ball, so it moves. As it rolls, friction turns the movement energy into a little heat and sound, which spread out, so the ball slows and stops. The energy is not lost, only changed.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is the Sun called the great source of energy for almost everything?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Because the Sun gives light and heat directly, and it is also the starting point for wind, for the growth of plants, and therefore for food. Most energy on Earth can be traced back to the Sun.

questions.question: Energy is best described as:
options: the ability to do work or cause change
questions.answer: the ability to do work or cause change
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the ability to do work or cause change. This is the scientific meaning of energy.

questions.question: Which type of energy does a running goat have?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: kinetic energy. Anything that moves, like a running goat, has kinetic (movement) energy.

questions.question: What type of energy is stored in a stretched rubber band?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: elastic potential energy. Stretching stores energy that is released when you let go.

questions.question: The greatest source of energy for the Earth is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Sun. Almost all energy on Earth begins with the Sun, including food and wind.

questions.question: Which of these is a renewable energy source?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solar energy. The Sun will not run out, so solar energy is renewable. Oil, coal and petrol are non-renewable.

questions.question: When a mango falls from a tree, its potential energy changes into:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: kinetic energy. As the mango falls, its stored (potential) energy becomes movement (kinetic) energy.

questions.question: Energy can be:
options: changed from one form to another
questions.answer: changed from one form to another
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: changed from one form to another. Energy is never created or destroyed, only changed or moved.

questions.question: What form of energy do we hear with our ears?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sound energy. We hear sound energy, which travels as vibrations through the air.

questions.question: A solar panel changes light energy into:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: electrical energy. A solar panel turns the Sun's light energy into electricity.

questions.question: Which food gives your body energy?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dates and bariis. Food holds chemical energy that our bodies use.

questions.question: Why does a rolling ball slow down and stop?
options: energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out
questions.answer: energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: its movement energy turns into heat and sound and spreads out. The energy is not destroyed, only scattered.

selfAssessment: I can explain what energy is and why it is needed for anything to happen.
I can name and describe the main forms of energy: light, heat, sound, movement (kinetic), stored (potential), and electrical energy.
I can identify different energy sources, such as the Sun, food, wind, moving water, charcoal, and fuel.
I can describe how energy is transferred from one object to another and how it changes from one form to another.
I can state the big rule of energy: energy cannot be created or destroyed, only changed or moved.
I can explain the difference between renewable energy (like solar and wind) and non-renewable energy (like oil and charcoal).
I can give practical examples of how to save energy in your own home and community.
I can investigate energy questions fairly and record what you observe.
